hook: The Baroque church where Chopin's heart is sealed inside a pillar.
facts: Chopin was buried at Père-Lachaise in Paris, but his heart was smuggled to Warsaw in a jar by his sister Ludwika to fulfil his dying wish.
The urn with his heart was immured in a pillar of the left nave in 1880, with a Carrara-marble epitaph by Leonardo Marconi unveiled 5 March 1880.
The heart of Nobel laureate novelist Władysław Reymont is sealed in another pillar (1929).
During WWII the heart was removed by the Germans; it returned to the church on 17 October 1945, the 96th anniversary of Chopin's death.
In 2014 the heart was secretly examined and found to be in very good condition, preserved in what is believed to be cognac.
legend: For about 20 years the priests, disapproving of Chopin's link to a French scandal, refused to display it and the heart lay forgotten in a church storeroom before being walled into the pillar.

sources.note: pl.wikipedia: history, Chopin and Reymont heart urns, 1880 epitaph, coords 52.238611, 21.016667
dzieje.pl: 2014 examination found the heart in very good condition

w: Chopin lies in Paris, but his heart came home — smuggled to Warsaw in a jar by his sister Ludwika and sealed into a pillar of this Baroque church in 1880. The Germans took it in the war; it returned in 1945. Checked in secret in 2014, it was found perfectly preserved, afloat in what they believe is cognac. Reymont's heart is here too.

hook: William Lindley's 1880s Victorian waterworks, still purifying Warsaw's water 140 years on.
facts: Built 1883–1886 under engineer William Heerlein Lindley (son of William Lindley), commissioned by Warsaw president Sokrates Starynkiewicz.
Clean water first reached Warsaw's taps on 3 July 1886.
Slow sand filters, clean-water reservoirs and raw-water settling tanks from the 19th century are still in use today.
It is considered the best-preserved and still-functioning historic industrial facility in Warsaw, recognised as a Pomnik Historii (Monument of History).
The site is bounded by Koszykowa, Krzywickiego, Filtrowa and Raszyńska streets; the 40-metre water tower was decommissioned in the 1930s.

sources.note: pl.wikipedia: construction dates, address ul. Koszykowa 81, coords 52.221667, 20.995278
City of Warsaw: Monument of History status

w: The waterworks that gave Warsaw clean water — William Lindley's son built it in the 1880s, and its slow sand filters and brick reservoirs from 1886 still do the job 140 years on. The best-preserved working industrial monument in the city, a Pomnik Historii behind Filtrowa Street. Victorian engineering, still flowing.

hook: The world's first poster museum, in Wilanów Palace's old riding school.
facts: Opened on 4 June 1968 as the world's first museum dedicated solely to poster art; a branch of the National Museum in Warsaw.
Housed in the former palace riding school (manège) at the Wilanów complex, designed by Francesco Maria Lanci (1848).
Holds roughly 62,000 works, including the Polish Poster School collection.
Collection includes posters by Pablo Picasso, Andy Warhol and Stasys Eidrigevičius.
Address: ul. Stanisława Kostki Potockiego 10/16.

sources.note: pl.wikipedia: world's first poster museum, opened 1968, riding-school building, coords 52.164306, 21.088178
culture.pl: Polish Poster School collection and significance

w: The world's first museum given over entirely to the poster, opened in 1968 in the old riding school of Wilanów Palace — fitting, in the country of the Polish Poster School. Sixty-odd thousand works, Picasso and Warhol among them. Out at the city's green southern edge, beside the palace. Where Poland's great graphic art hangs framed.

hook: Warsaw's oldest bazaar — Praga's legendary 1880s marketplace where 'they had everything'.
facts: Founded in 1882 by the wealthy Warsaw pharmacist Julian Różycki, who had begun buying the plots eight years earlier.
Located at ul. Targowa 54, between Targowa and Brzeska streets in Praga-Północ.
It is the oldest surviving bazaar in Warsaw and one of the oldest markets in the city, inseparable from right-bank Praga's identity.
Its manager Manas Ryba pioneered covered, rentable stalls for street merchants — a novel idea at the time.
Famous in the People's Republic era as a place to buy goods unavailable in state shops; nicknamed 'Różyc'.

sources.note: pl.wikipedia: founded 1882 by Julian Różycki, ul. Targowa 54, coords 52.253056, 21.041250
warszawa.naszemiasto: cultural history of the bazaar

w: Warsaw's oldest bazaar, founded in 1882 by a Praga pharmacist — and the place that, in the grey People's Republic, 'had everything' the state shops didn't. Praga calls it 'Różyc.' Its manager invented the covered, rentable stall here. Battered, shrunken, still trading off Targowa. The right bank's heart.

hook: A 1904 riverside power station reborn as one of Europe's best food halls.
facts: Launched in November 1904 by the Paris-based Compagnie d'Électricité de Varsovie; built 1903–1905 with its own water intake from the Vistula.
Originally had sixteen boilers and five turbine sets, the largest rated at 4,000 kW.
The complex (between Wybrzeże Kościuszkowskie, Tamka, Dobra and Leszczyńska streets) is in the heritage register.
Reopened in 2020 as an open retail and food complex preserving the historic steel boiler-house structure.
Its Food Hall was named by National Geographic Traveller (UK) as the best in Poland and one of six most interesting in Europe.

sources.note: pl.wikipedia: launched 1904, history, heritage status, coords 52.239722, 21.028611
National Geographic: food hall among 6 best in Europe

w: A riverside power station from 1904, its boilers drawing water straight from the Vistula to light the city — and, since 2020, one of Europe's best food halls under its preserved steel frame. National Geographic Traveller called it Poland's finest. The turbines, swapped for tasting menus. Eat where the city got its current.

hook: One of Europe's largest roof gardens, draped over the University Library.
facts: Opened on 12 June 2002, designed by landscape architect Irena Bajerska, atop the Warsaw University Library on ul. Dobra 56/66.
One of the largest and most beautiful roof gardens in Europe, spanning more than one hectare with about 5,111 m² of planting.
Split into an upper and lower garden linked by a stream and cascading water, divided into golden, silver, crimson and green sections.
Free to enter and open from spring to late autumn.
The library building itself is fronted by Marek Budzyński and Zbigniew Badowski's celebrated green architecture.

sources.note: Official BUW: roof garden opened 2002, address ul. Dobra 56/66, ~17,000 m², coords 52.242514, 21.024457
University of Warsaw: one of the most beautiful roof gardens

w: One of Europe's largest roof gardens, draped over the University Library on Powiśle — more than a hectare of planting in coloured quarters, laced with a stream that cascades off the roof. Irena Bajerska laid it out in 2002; it is free, and open spring to autumn. Climb up for the green and the view over the Vistula.

hook: Poland's first workers' housing colony — raw-brick barracks with a teetotal rulebook.
facts: Built 1898–1900, funded by a donation from Hipolit and Ludwika Wawelberg marking the 50th anniversary of the H. Wawelberg Bank House.
One of the first low-cost workers' housing estates in all of Poland, in the Młynów area of Wola; architect Edward Goldberg.
Three identical multi-family blocks in 'barracks' style, built of bare brick and left unplastered to this day.
Residents lived under a strict regulation that banned alcohol and keeping animals.
The estate had a school, reading room, medical centre, playroom and daycare; located at ul. Górczewska 15/15A and ul. Wawelberga 3.

sources.note: pl.wikipedia: built 1898–1900, founders, raw-brick blocks, coords 52.236579, 20.968148
urbnews.pl: Warsaw's oldest housing estate, design and social program

w: Poland's first workers' housing — three bare-brick barracks blocks the Wawelberg bankers built in 1900 for Wola's poor, never plastered to this day. They came with a school, a reading room, a clinic and a creche — and rules, strict ones, banning drink and animals. Charity with a rulebook. Raw brick, a hundred and twenty years on.
